content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 2, 2016

Leon J. Page
Orange County Counsel
P.O. Box 1379
Santa Ana, CA 92702-1379

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-037

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Orange County Board of
Supervisors and Mike Ryan, the Director of the Orange County Social Services Agency ("SSA"),
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section
1090. We do not provide advice on other conflict of interest restrictions, if any, that could arise
such as those governed by the common law. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), meaning that any advice we provide assumes the facts you
provide are complete and accurate. Also, please note, the Commission will not advise with respect
to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A)) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this
letter apply only to prospective actions.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTIONS²

Under Section 1090, may the county enter into contracts to provide services for
Commercially Sexually Exploited Children ("CSEC") with the employers of individuals who serve
on a steering committee (the "Steering Committee") or multidisciplinary team (the "MDT") that
recommends to the Social Services Agency ("SSA") which services to fund?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Your questions are phrased in terms of whether the agencies and private entities may participate on the
Steering Committee and MDT. Holding a position or serving on a governmental body does not of itself violate the
conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090. Our analysis, therefore, addresses the participation of Steering
Committee and MDT members in decisions regarding the contracts.

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 2

2. Under the Act, may these individuals participate in decisions to recommend to SSA which
services to fund?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. The MDT's and Steering Committees' enabling legislation contemplates that the
entities represented on the MDT and Steering Committee would bid on the contracts.

2. Yes. These individuals are not public officials under the Act

FACTS³

ORANGE COUNTY SOCIAL SERVICES AGENCY (SSA) PROGRAM FOR COMMERCIALLY SEXUALLY
EXPLOITED CHILDREN

In 2014, the state legislature amended Welfare and Institutions Code 16524.6 et seq. to
establish a state-funded program for counties to provide specialized services for Commercially
Sexually Exploited Children ("CSEC"). The purpose of the program is to encourage, and sometimes
require, collaboration between public agencies and private organizations to work together to
identify and provide the most appropriate services to CSEC children. The California Department of
Social Services administers the state program and selects the local agencies and organizations that
will receive the funds.

The Plan: Orange County elected to participate in the program and on June 30, 2015,
submitted a Plan (the "Plan") to the state describing how the County intended to use the funds. The
Plan also describes the County's current approaches to address the needs of the County's CSEC
population. These approaches involve a number of County departments and other service providers
that often work together.

The local entities specifically agree to participate in the Steering Committee and help
establish services of the CSEC population. The Plan labeled these entities "ex officio" members of
the Steering Committee. You understand that the intent was for their participation to be non-voting
members.

The MOU: Pursuant to the Legislation and the Plan, on September 25, 2015, the County
prepared a nonbinding Memorandum of Understanding (the "MOU") between SSA, the County's
Health Care Agency, Juvenile Court and Probation Department (the "Parties"). The MOU sets forth
the "interagency protocol" for implementing the program. The MOU also outlines the duties and
responsibilities of the Parties, allows them to function as a team in the planning and implementation
of the program, contains program content and purpose, and sets forth specific guidelines for
implementing and administering the program. The terms of the MOU are almost entirely based on a
suggested template (the "Template") provided by the state.⁴

³ In addition to the materials you have provided, we have considered facts found in the All County Information
Notice No. I-23-15 issued by the Department of Social Services of the California Health and Human Services Agency.

⁴ See All County Letter No. 15-48 dated May 29, 2015.

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 3

The Model Interagency Protocol Framework (the "Model Framework"): This is the state's
guide that counties may use in developing their own interagency protocol. (The protocol is part of
the MOU.)5

MDT

Welfare and Institution Code sections 16524.6 et seq.

The statute requires the county to form a multidisciplinary team. The MDT is charged with
assessing each child's needs, providing individualized case management and service planning, and
delivering the services to each child. The Plan is required to include "documentation indicating the
County's collaboration with County partner agencies and children-focused organizations, which
shall include the formation of a multidisciplinary team."

The MOU and Plan

The statute requires that the Plan include "documentation indicating the County's
collaboration with partner agencies and children-focused entities, which shall include the formation
of a multidisciplinary team to serve [CSEC] children." The MOU contains a provision that
designates membership on the MDT. The MDT must include a representative from each Party and,
in addition, may include certain other county departments6 and private community service
providers.7

The Model Framework

The Model Framework sets forth the characteristics that members of a multidisciplinary
team must have. They must be individuals that have CSEC training in preventing, identifying,
managing and treating abused and neglected children, and who are qualified to provide a broad
range of services for them.

MDT Recommendations

You state that the MDT does not make recommendations regarding the award of contracts nor does
it participate in preparing Requests for Proposal or the contracts. The MDT as a team makes
recommendations to SSA for the care and service need of each child and, SSA, after independently
deciding which services to fund, procures contracts in accordance with the County's established
procurement protocols.

5 See Welf & Inst. Code, section 18961.7, subd. (b)(1) and "All County Information Notice No. I-23-15"
dated April 7, 2015.

6 County's Children and Family Services, Emergency Response, social workers, law enforcement, minor's
counsel, probation officers, juvenile court, Behavioral Health Care Agency and Public Health Nursing.

7 Vanguard University; Crittenton Services for Children & Families; New Alternatives Inc.; Community
Service Programs, Inc.; and The Teen Project.

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 4

Steering Committee

The MOU and Plan

A steering committee is not expressly mentioned in the Welfare and Institutions Code. However, the state's Template includes a provision for a steering committee to oversee the CSEC Program.⁸ The MOU was almost entirely based on the Template which includes a provision requiring a steering committee. The MOU contains this provision.

In accordance with the terms of the MOU, the Parties convened a meeting with representatives from ten County agencies⁹ and several private organizations.¹⁰ You state that the members of the group were not involved in the drafting of the Plan and that their role was limited to providing suggestions and expressing concerns. The MOU further provides that the Steering Committee may be co-chaired by SSA's Director of Children and Family Services (as lsd), a member of the Board of Supervisors and the Presiding Judge of Juvenile Court.

Steering Committee Recommendations

You state that the Steering Committee may make a recommendation for a service but that SSA independently considers these recommendations and selects the services to fund. SSA then issues an RFP and prepares a contract in accordance with the County's procurement and contracting requirements. The contract is then submitted to the Board of Supervisors for approval. The Steering Committee does not make recommendations to SSA or the Board of Supervisors regarding the awarding of contracts.

FUTURE CONTRACTS WITH THE COUNTY

Approximately two dozen government agencies and private organizations participate in the MDT and the Steering Committee. Some of these participants would like to enter into contracts with SSA or apply for grants to provide services identified in the Plan (the "Proposed Contracts"). As stated above, the members of the Steering Committee and the MDT only make recommendations regarding services needed as contemplated under state law and the state's Template. Once services have been recommended, the members of the Steering Committee and the MDT do not make any further recommendations to SSA or the Board of Supervisors regarding the awarding of contracts.

_______________

⁸ See Department of Social Services – All County Information Notice No. 15-48.

⁹ Probation, Probation, Health Care Agency, (which includes Behavioral Health, Alcohol & Drug Services, and Public Health), Juvenile Court, County Counsel, District Attorney's Office, Public Defender's office; Minor Counsel; Juvenile Justice Commission; Anaheim Police Department (which includes Human Trafficking Law Enforcement Task Force), and Department of Education.

¹⁰ These are the same private organizations listed in footnote 7.

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 5

ANALYSIS

Section 1090

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "fm members of the Legislature, state, County, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are members." Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies.11 The Section 1090 prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties.12 A contract that violates Section 1090 is void.

Section 1090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member bodies, whether elected or appointed. The Section 1090 prohibition also applies to members of advisory bodies and independent contractors where they have a potential to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency.13 This is because such individuals can influence development of a contract during preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids so long as those actions take place in his or her official, rather than private capacity.14

Section 1090 covers a broad range of actions by a public official other than actually approving a contract, including actions involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids,15 so long as those actions take place in his or her official, rather than private capacity. In addition, Section 1090 applies not only to individuals but to corporations. (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261.)

MDT

You have asked us to specifically address a narrow exception articulated in the Attorney General Opinion ((1995) 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 362) to the prohibitions of Section 1090. The exception was applied to public officers whose membership on an advisory body was mandated by the enabling legislation. Under the facts before the Attorney General, a city's community redevelopment agency had amended a redevelopment plan for a proposed 42-acre development and a 25-member project area committee was formed to provide advice on policy matters relating to the proposed development. The issue before the Attorney General was whether the committee could

11 Stigall v. Tafi (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.

12 Id. at pp. 646-649.)

13 Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278; City Council v. McKinley (1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204.); Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1114, 1124-1125; Willis Advice Letter. No. A-15-163.

14 (People v. Sobel (1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.)

15 Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237, see also Stigall v. City of Tafi, supra at 569.

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 6

give its advice to the agency where several of the committee members owned property within the
project area. The Opinion concluded that the members' potential conflicts of interest did not
preclude them from advising the agency.

The Opinion said that the Community Redevelopment Act governing redevelopment
agencies was enacted "to protect and promote the sound development and redevelopment of
blighted areas and the general welfare of the inhabitants of communities in which they exist." The
statute required that residents and existing community organizations within the project area be
represented on the project area committee and that membership be limited to residential owner
occupants, residential tenants, business owners, and existing organizations within the project area.
The redevelopment agency was required to obtain the advice of the committee regarding policy
matters that would affect the project area. The statute further required that all redevelopment plans
be submitted to the committee before adoption by the redevelopment agency.

Several committee members owned property in the project area, as required to meet the
statutory qualifications for them to be committee members. Some of these owners could eventually
sell their properties to the agency or developer or enter into development agreements directly with
the agency. The Opinion stated that "In effect, the Legislature has designated persons who have a
financial interest in a redevelopment project area to be advisers to the redevelopment agency
concerning project matters that would affect the advisers as property owners . . .. To apply the
general prohibition of section 1090 in such circumstances or to require the financially interested
PAC members to abstain would undermine the Legislature's express determination that property
owners in the project area are to render advice as members of the PAC."

The facts you have provided are similar. Welfare and Institutions Code, section 18961.7,
subd. (b)(1), and the state's Model Framework require that the local agency receiving funds form a
"multidisciplinary team" that has CSEC training. A multidisciplinary team is defined as "any team
of two or more persons who are trained in the prevention, identification, management, or treatment
of child abuse or neglect cases and who are qualified to provide a broad range of services related to
child abuse or neglect." (Emphasis added.) In addition, the state-approved MOU, lists specific
agencies and private entities that may serve on the MDT.

It is apparent that these agencies, individuals and private organizations all play important
roles and provide essential services for CSEC. This is the very reason they were singled out for
membership on the MDT. In many cases their roles and services are unique. For example, only the
Juvenile Court, (which is one of the listed agencies), adjudicates matters relating to CSEC. To deny
funds to the Juvenile Court or other agencies or private entities identified in the MOU would be
inconsistent with the Legislature's intent in funding these types of activities. We do not believe that
the Legislature contemplated such a result. Accordingly, SSA is not prohibited from entering into
Proposed Contracts with the agencies and private entities that are represented on the MDT.

STEERING COMMITTEE

We address the question of whether the exception for members of advisory bodies whose
membership is mandated by the enabling legislation also applies to the Steering Committee. We
found that the MDT fell within the exception. The facts here are similar with one exception. A
steering committee is not expressly mentioned in the Welfare and Institutions Code. However, the

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 7

state's suggested MOU Template includes a provision for a steering committee and the county's
MOU therefore includes a provision requiring a steering committee.

We find that by including the Steering Committee requirement in the Template, the state
contemplated that a steering committee would be formed and that it would include members who
have a financial interest in the contract. As is the case with the MDT, the advisory role of the
Steering Committee includes making recommendations for needed services. The MOU expressly
lists agencies and organizations that may serve on the Steering Committee. These agencies and
organizations all play important roles and provide essential services for CSEC. This is the very
reason they were singled out for membership on the MDT. We believe that by including a
requirement for a steering committee in the Template and approving the MOU that contains the
requirement, the state contemplated that agencies and organizations serving on the Steering
Committee because of their expertise would be permitted to enter into the subject contracts.
Otherwise, the Legislature's purpose in adopting a program for funding the needs of CSEC might
be thwarted. Therefore, we find that under the exception, employers of the individuals serving on
the Steering Committee may enter into the contracts with SSA to provide CSEC services.

Political Reform Act

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. In the current matter, the
individuals serving on the MDT and Steering Committee on behalf of their employers have an
interest in their employers as a source of income.60 (Section 87103(c) and (d).)

The threshold inquiry is whether, under the Act, these individuals are "public officials."
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state
or local government agency. For the purpose of further defining "public official," Regulation
18700(e)(2) defines "member" as follows:

"(2) "Member" does not include an individual who performs duties as part of a
committee, board, commission, group, or other body that does not have
decisionmaking authority.

"(A) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body possesses
decisionmaking authority whenever:

"(i) It may make a final governmental decision;

"(ii) It may compel or prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an
exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be
overridden; or

60 In addition, members who are employed by a private for-profit entity have a financial interest in a business
entity.

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 8

"(iii) It makes substantive recommendations and, over an extended period of time, those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by another public official or governmental agency.

"(B) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body does not possess decisionmaking authority if it is formed or engaged for the sole purpose of researching a topic and preparing a report or recommendation for submission to another public official or governmental agency that has final decisionmaking authority, and does not meet any of the criteria set forth in subsection (2)(A) (i-iii)."

Thus, the MDT and Steering Committee may be deemed to have decisionmaking authority if they have the ability to (1) make a final decision, (2) compel or prevent a decision, or (3) make substantive recommendations that are, over an extended period, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification.

MDT

You state that the MDT is tasked with outlining the duties and responsibilities of the Parties to function as a team in the planning and implementation of the Program. Children are referred to the MDT for assessment and the MDT coordinates individualized case management, service planning and delivery of services for each child. Other than making recommendations regarding services that need funding, the MDT does not make recommendations to SSA or the Board of Supervisors regarding the awarding of contracts. Also, the MDT has only met once. Under these facts, the MDT does not make the final decision to award a contract, does not compel or prevent the decision, nor has it made substantive recommendations that have been, over an extended period, been regularly approved by SSA or the Board of Supervisors. Therefore, we conclude that the members of the MDT are not public officials and are not subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.

Steering Committee

You state that the Steering Committee was formed to oversee the CSEC Program which includes expressing its members' concerns and making recommendations to SSA regarding the Plan. However, SSA alone was responsible for drafting the Plan. You also state that after SSA adopted the Plan, the Steering Committee became responsible for developing the Plan and assuring collaboration among the Parties and other participating agencies and entities to better identify and serve CSEC children. This involves identifying needed services and recommending the services that are most in need of funding. SSA then considers the recommendations, and other information from a variety of sources, and independently sends. SSA issues an RFP and prepares a contract in accordance with the County's procurement and contracting requirements. The contract is then submitted to the Board of Supervisors for approval.

Like the MDT, the Steering Committee does not make recommendations to SSA or the Board of Supervisors regarding the awarding of contracts other than recommending the services that need funding. Also, you state that the Steering Committee has met six times, the first meeting being on August 26, 2015. We believe that this short of period of time does not constitute an

File No. A-16-037
Page No. 9

"extended period of time" for making substantive recommendations that have been regularly
approved by SSA or the Board of Supervisors. Under these facts, the Steering Committee does not
make the final decision to award a contract, does not compel or prevent the decision, nor has it
made substantive recommendations that have been, over an extended period of time, regularly
approved by SSA or the Board of Supervisors. Therefore, we find that the members of the Steering
Committee are not public officials and are not subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions.

Conclusion: Section 1090 does not prohibit the County from entering into contracts to provide
CSEC services to the employers of individuals serving on the MDT or the Steering Committee and
these individuals are not prohibited by the Act's conflict of interest provisions from participating in
decisions to recommend services for funding.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

VJ:jgl

sections.analysis:
Section 1090 
File No. A-16-03 7  
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m)embers of the Legislature, state, County, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by anybody or board of which they are members." 
Section I 090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that 
prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the 
best interests of their agencies.1 1  The Section 1090 prohibition applies regardless of whether the 
terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. I:! A contract that violates Section I 090 is 
void. 
Section I 090 applies to virtually all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member 
bodies, whether elected or appointed. The Section I 090 prohibition also applies to members of 
advisory bodies and independent contractors where they have a potential to exert considerable 
influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency.13 This is because such individuals can 
influence development of a contract during preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, 
reasoning, planning, drawing plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids so long as those 
actions take place in his or her official, rather than private capacity.14 
Section I 090 covers a broad range of actions by a public official other than actually 
approving a contract, including actions involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, 
compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids, 1 5  so 
long as those actions take place in his or her official, rather than private capacity. In addition, 
Section I 090 applies not only to individuals but to corporations. (Dm•is , .. Frcs110 U11(/icd School 
District (2015) 23 7 Cal.App.4th 261.) 
MDT 
You have asked us to specifically address a narrow exception articulated in the Attorney 
General Opinion (( I 995) 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 362) to the prohibitions of Section 1 090. The 
exception was applied to public officers whose membership on an advisory body was mandated by 
the enabling legislation. Under the facts before the Attorney General, a city's community 
redevelopment agency had amended a redevelopment plan for a proposed 42-acre development and 
a 25-member project area committee was formed to provide advice on policy matters relating to the 
proposed development. The issue before the Attorney General was whether the committee could 
11 Stigall 1·. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569. 
1 Id. at pp. 646-649.) 
ll Schaefer 1·. Berinstein { 1956) 140 Cal.App.2d 278: City Council 1•. McKinley (l 978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204.); 
Huh City Solid Waste Se1,·ic:es, Inc. 1·. City of Compton (2010) 186 Cal.App.4th 1 1 14. 1 124-1 125; Willis Ad\'ice Letter. 
No. AAIS-163. 
•Ÿ (People 1·. Sobel ( 1974) 40 Cal.App.3d 1046, 1052.) 
IS Millbrae Assn. for &siclential S111,fral 1·. City ofi\,fillbrcte ( /968J 262 Cc,/ App . .2cl 111. 137: see also Stigall 
1·. Cin· of Taft. supra at 569. 


File No. A-16-03 7  
give its advice to the agency where several of the committee members owned property within the 
project area. The Opinion concluded that the members' potential conflicts of interest did not 
preclude them from advising the agency. 
The Opinion said that the Community Redevelopment Act governing redevelopment 
agencies was enacted "to protect and promote the sound development and redevelopment of 
blighted areas and the general welfare of the inhabitants of communities in which they exist." The 
statute required that residents and existing community organizations within the project area be 
represented on the project area committee and that membership be limited to residential owner 
occupants, residential tenants, business owners, and existing organizations within the project area. 
The redevelopment agency was required to obtain the advice of the committee regarding policy 
matters that would affect the project area. The statute further required that all redevelopment plans 
be submitted to the committee before adoption by the redevelopment agency. 
Several committee members owned property in the project area, as required to meet the 
statutory qualifications for them to be committee members. Some of these owners could eventually 
sell their properties to the agency or developer or enter into development agreements directly with 
the agency. The Opinion stated that "In effect, the Legislature has designated persons who have a 
financial interest in a redevelopment project area to be advisers to the redevelopment agency 
concerning project matters that would affect the advisers as property owners . . .. To apply the 
general prohibition of section I 090 in such circumstances or to require the financially interested 
PAC members to abstain would undennine the Legislature's express detennination that property 
owners in the project area are to render advice as members of the PAC." 
The facts you have provided are similar. Welfare and Institutions Code, section 18961.7, 
subd. (b)( I ), and the state's Model Framework require that the local agency receiving funds form a 
"multidisciplinary team" that has CSEC training. A multidisciplinary team is defined as "any team 
of two or more persons who are trained in the prevention, identification, management, or treatment 
of child abuse or neglect cases and who are qual(fied to prol'ide a broad range of sen·iccs related to 
child abuse or neglect." (Emphasis added.) In addition, the state-approved MOU, lists specific 
agencies and private entities that may serve on the MDT. 
It is apparent that these agencies, individuals and private organizations all play important 
roles and provide essential services for CSEC. This is the very reason they were singled out for 
membership on the MDT. In many cases their roles and services are unique. For example, only the 
Juvenile Court, (which is one of the listed agencies), adjudicates matters relating to CSEC. To deny 
funds to the Juvenile Court or other agencies or private entities identified in the MOU would be 
inconsistent with the Legislature's intent in funding these types of activities. We do not believe that 
the Legislature contemplated such a result. Accordingly, SSA is not prohibited from entering into 
Proposed Contracts with the agencies and private entities that are represented on the MDT. 
STEERING COMMITTEE 
We address the question of whether the exception for members of advisory bodies whose 
membership is mandated by the enabling legislation also applies to the Steering Committee. We 
found that the MDT fell within the exception. The facts here are similar with one exception. A 
steering committee is not expressly mentioned in the Welfare and Institutions Code. However, the 


File No. A-1 6-037  
state's suggested MOU Template includes a provision for a steering committee and the county's 
MOU therefore includes a provision requiring a steering committee. 
We find that by including the Steering Committee requirement in the Template, the state 
contemplated that a steering committee would be formed and that it would include members who 
have a financial interest in the contract. As is the case with the MDT, the advisory role of the 
Steering Committee includes making recommendations for needed services. The MOU expressly 
lists agencies and organizations that may serve on the Steering Committee. These agencies and 
organizations all play important roles and provide essential services for CSEC. This is the very 
reason they were singled out for membership on the MDT. We believe that by including a 
requirement for a steering committee in the Template and approving the MOU that contains the 
requirement, the state contemplated that agencies and organizations serving on the Steering 
Committee because of their expertise would be permitted to enter into the subject contracts. 
Otherwise, the Legislature's purpose in adopting a program for funding the needs of CSEC might 
be thwarted. Therefore, we find that under the exception, employers of the individuals serving on 
the Steering Committee may enter into the contracts with SSA to provide CSEC services. 
Political Reform Act 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. In the current matter, the 
individuals serving on the MDT and Steering Committee on behalf of their employers have an 
interest in their employers as a source of income.16 (Section 87103(c) and (d).) 
The threshold inquiry is whether, under the Act, these individuals are .. public officials." 
Section 82048 defines "public official" as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state 
or local government agency. For the purpose of further defining "public official," Regulation 
l 8700(c)(2) defines "member'' as follows: 
entity. 
"(2) "Member" does not include an individual who performs duties as part of a 
committee, board, commission, &rroup, or other body that does not have 
decisionmaking authority. 
"(A) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body possesses 
decisionmaking authority whenever: 
"(i) It may make a final governmental decision; 
"(ii) It may compel or prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an 
exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be 
overridden; or 
11' In addition. members who are employed by a private for-profit entity ha\'e a financial interest in a business 


File No. A-1 6-037  
"(iii) It makes substantive recommendations and, over an extended period of time, 
those recommendations have been regularly approved without significant 
amendment or modification by another public official or governmental agency. 
"{B) A committee, board, commission, group, or other body does not possess 
decisionmaking authority if it is formed or engaged for the sole purpose of 
researching a topic and preparing a report or recommendation for submission to 
another public official or governmental agency that has final decisionmaking 
authority, and does not meet any of the criteria set forth in subsection (2)(A) (i­
iii)." 
Thus, the MDT and Steering Committee may be deemed to have decisionmaking authority 
if they have the ability to (I) make a final decision, (2) compel or prevent a decision, or (3) make 
substantive recommendations that are, over an extended period, regularly approved without 
significant amendment or modification. 
MDT 
You state that the MDT is tasked with outlining the duties and responsibilities of the Parties 
to function as a team in the planning and implementation of the Program. Children are referred to 
the MDT for assessment and the MDT coordinates individualized case management, service 
planning and delivery of services for each child. Other than making recommendations regarding 
services that need funding, the MDT does not make recommendations to SSA or the Board of 
Supervisors regarding the awarding of contracts. Also, The MDT has only met once. Under these 
facts, the MDT docs not make the final decision to award a contract, does not compel or prevent the 
decision, nor has it made substantive recommendations that have been, over an extended period, 
been regularly approved by SSA or the Board of Supervisors. Therefore, we conclude that the 
members of the MDT are not public officials and arc not subject to the Act's conflict of interest 
prov1s10ns. 
Steering Committee 
You state that the Steering Committee was formed to oversee the CSEC Pro!,rram which 
includes expressing its members' concerns and making recommendations to SSA regarding the 
Plan. However, SSA alone was responsible for drafting the Plan. You also state that after SSA 
adopted the Plan, the Steering Committee became responsible for developing the Plan and assuring 
collaboration among the Parties and other participating agencies and entities to better identify and 
serve CSEC children. This involves identifying needed services and recommending the services that 
are most in need of funding. SSA then considers the recommendations, and other information from 
a variety of sources, and independently selects the services to fund. SSA issues an RFP and 
prepares a contract in accordance with the County's procurement and contracting requirements. The 
contract is then submitted to the Board of Supervisors for approval. 
Like the MDT, the Steering Committee does not make recommendations to SSA or the 
Board of Supervisors regarding the awarding of contracts other than recommending the services 
that need funding. Also, you state that the Steering Committee has met six times, the first meeting 
being on August 26, 201 5. We believe that this short of period of time does not constitute an 


File No. A-16-03 7  
"extended period of time" for making substantive recommendations that have been regularly 
approved by SSA or the Board of Supervisors. Under these facts, the Steering Committee does not 
make the final decision to award a contract, does not compel or prevent the decision, nor has it 
made substantive recommendations that have been, over an extended period of time, regularly 
approved by SSA or the Board of Supervisors. Therefore, we find that the members of the Steering 
Committee are not public officials and are not subject to the Act's conflict of interest provisions. 
Conclusion: Section l 090 does not prohibit the County from entering into contracts to provide 
CSEC services to the employers of individuals serving on the MDT or the Steering Committee and 
these individuals are not prohibited by the Act's conflict of interest provisions from participating in 
decisions to recommend services for funding.

QUESTION: S 
Under Section I 090, may the county enter into contracts to provide services for 
Commercially Sexually Exploited Children ("CSEC") with the employers of individuals who serve 
on a steering committee (the "Steering Committee") or multidisciplinary team (the "MDT") that 
recommends to the Social Services Agency ("SSA") which services to fund?1 Your questions are phrased in terms of whether the agencies and pri\·ate entities may participate on the 
Steering Committee and MDT. Holding a position or serving on a governmental body does not of itself violate the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Sect10n 1090. Our analysis. therefore. addresses the participation of Steering 
Committee and MDT members in decisions regarding the contracts. 


File No. A-16-03 7  
2. Under the Act, may these individuals participate in decisions to recommend to SSA which 
services to fund?

CONCLUSION: I .  Yes. The MOT's and Steering Committees' enabling legislation contemplates that the 
entities represented on the MDT and Steering Committee would bid on the contracts. 
2. Yes. These individuals are not public officials under the Act 
FACTS3 
ORANGE COUNTY SOCIAL SER\'ICES AGENCY (SSA) PROGRAM FOR COMMERCIALLY SEXUALLY 
EXPLOITED CHILDREN 
In 2014, the state legislature amended Welfare and Institutions Code 16524.6 et seq. to 
establish a state-funded program for counties to provide specialized services for Commercially 
Sexually Exploited Children ("CSEC''). The purpose of the program is to encourage, and sometimes 
require, collaboration between public agencies and private organizations to work together to 
identify and provide the most appropriate services to CSEC children. The California Department of 
Social Services administers the state program and selects the local agencies and organizations that 
will receive the funds. 
The Plan: Orange County elected to participate in the program and on June 30, 2015, 
submitted a Plan (the "Plan") to the state describing how the County intended to use the funds. The 
Plan also describes the County's current approaches to address the needs of the County's CSEC 
population. These approaches involve a number of County departments and other service providers 
that often work together. 
The local entities specifically agree to participate in the Steering Committee and help 
establish services of the CSEC population. The Plan labeled these entities "ex officio" members of 
the Steering Committee. You understand that the intent was for their participation to be non-voting 
members. 
The 1'v!OU: Pursuant to the Legislation and the Plan, on September 25, 2015, the County 
prepared a nonbinding Memorandum of Understanding (the "MOU") between SSA, the County's 
Health Care Agency, Juvenile Court and Probation Department {the "Parties"). The MOU sets forth 
the "interagency protocol" for implementing the program. The MOU also outlines the duties and 
responsibilities of the Parties, allows them to function as a team in the planning and implementation 
of the program, contains program content and purpose, and sets forth specific guidelines for 
implementing and administering the program. The terms of the MOU are almost entirely based on a 
suggested template (the "Template") provided by the state.4 
) In addition to the materials you have provided. we have considered facts found in the All County lnfonnation 
Notice No. 1·23•15 issued by the Department of Social Services of the California Health and Human Services Agency. 
ŷ See All County Letter No. 15-48 dated May 29, 2015.  
The Model /11terage11cy Protocol Framelmrk (the ",Hodel Framell'ork "): This is the state's 
guide that counties may use in developing their own interagency protocol. (The protocol is part of 
the MOU.)5 
MDT 
We((are and Institution Code sections J 6524. 6 et seq. 
The statute requires the county to form a multidisciplinary team. The MDT is charged with 
assessing each child's needs, providing individualized case management and service planning, and 
delivering the services to each child. The Plan is required to include "documentation indicating the 
County's collaboration with County partner agencies and children-focused organizations, which 
shall include the formation of a multidisciplinary team." 
The MOU and Plan 
The statute requires that the Plan include "documentation indicating the County's 
collaboration with partner agencies and children-focused entities, which shall include the formatiqn 
of a multidisciplinary team to serve (CSEC] children." The MOU contains a provision that 
designates membership on the MDT. The MDT must include a representative from each Party and, 
in addition, may include certain other county departments6 and private community service 
providers. 7 
The Model Framelmrk. 
The Model Framework sets forth the characteristics that members of a multidisciplinary 
team must have. They must be individuals that have CSEC training in preventing, identifying, 
managing and treating abused and neglected children, and who arc qualified to provide a broad 
range of services for them. 
MDT Recomme11datio11s 
You state that the MDT does not make recommendations regarding the award of contracts nor does 
it participate in preparing Requests for Proposal or the contracts. The MDT as a team makes 
recommendations to SSA for the care and service need of each child and, SSA, after independently 
deciding which services to fund, procures contracts in accordance with the County's established 
procurement protocols. 
s See Welf. & Inst. Code. sectmn I 8961.7. subd. (b)( I) and "All County lnfonnation Nottce No. I-23-I 5" 
da1ed April 7. 2015. 
6 County's Children and Family Sen ices, Emergency Response, social workers, law enforcemenL minor·s 
counsel, probation officers, ju\ enile court, Behavioral Health Care Agency and Public Health Nursing. 
7 Vanguard University; Crittenton Services for Children & Families; New Altemauves Inc.; Community 
Sen•ice Programs. Inc.; and The Teen Project. 


Steering Committee 
The MOU and Plan  
A steering committee is not expressly mentioned in the Welfare and Institutions Code. 
However, the state's Template includes a provision for a steering committee to oversee the CSEC 
Program.8 The MOU was almost entirely based on the Template which includes a provision 
requiring a steering committee. The MOU contains this provision. 
In accordance with the tenns of the MOU, the Parties convened a meeting with 
representatives from ten County agencies9 and several private organizations.10 You state that the 
members of the &lfoup were not involved in the drafting of the Plan and that their role was limited to 
providing suggestions and expressing concerns. The MOU further provides that the Steering 
Committee may be co-chaired by SSA's Director of Children and Family Services (as lead), a 
member of the Board of Supervisors and the Presiding Judge of Juvenile Court. 
Steering Commillec Rccommc11dations 
You state that the Steering Committee may make a recommendation for a service but that 
SSA independently considers these recommendations and selects the services to fund. SSA then 
issues an RFP and prepares a contract in accordance with the County's procurement and contracting 
requirements. The contract is then submitted to the Board of Supervisors for approval. The Steering 
Committee does not make recommendations to SSA or the Board of Supervisors regarding the 
awarding of contracts. 
FUTURE CONTRACTS WITII THE COUNTY 
Approximately two dozen government agencies and private organizations participate in the 
MDT and the Steering Committee. Some of these participants would like to enter into contracts 
with SSA or apply for grants to provide services identified in the Plan (the "Proposed Contracts"). 
As stated above, the members of the Steering Committee and the MDT only make 
recommendations regarding services needed as contemplated under state law and the state's 
Template. Once services have been recommended, the members of the Steering Committee and the 
MDT do not make any further recommendations to SSA or the Board of Supervisors regarding the 
awarding of contracts. 
8 See Department of Social Services - All County Infonnation Notice No. 15-48. 
'1 Probation, Probation. Health Care Agency, (which includes Behavioral Health, Alcohol & Drug Ser\'ices. and 
Public Health), Ju\'enilc Court, County Counsel, District Allorney's Office, Public Defender's office; Minor Counsel: 
Juvenile Justice Commission; Anaheim Police Department (which includes Human Trafficking Law Enforcement Task 
Force), and Department of Education. 
Ill These are the same private organizations listed in footnote 7.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 2, 2016 Leon J. Page Orange County Counsel P.O. Box 1379 Santa Ana, CA 92702-1379 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-037 This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Orange County Board of Supervisors and Mike Ryan, the Director of the Orange County Social Services Agency ("SSA"), regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ and Section 1090. We do not provide advice on other conflict of interest restrictions, if any, that could arise such as those governed by the common law. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), meaning that any advice we provide assumes the facts you provide are complete and accurate. Also, please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A)) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Orange County District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).) QUESTIONS² Under Section 1090, may the county enter into contracts to provide services for Commercially Sexually Exploited Children ("CSEC") with the employers of individuals who serve on a steering committee (the "Steering Committee") or multidisciplinary team (the "MDT") that recommends to the Social Services Agency ("SSA") which services to fund? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Your questions are phrased in terms of whether the agencies and private entities may participate on the Steering Committee and MDT. Holding a position or serving on a governmental body does not of itself violate the conflict of interest provisions of the Act or Section 1090. Our analysis, therefore, addresses the participation of Steering Committee and MDT members in decisions regarding the contracts. File No. A-16-037 Page No. 2 2. Under the Act, may these individuals participate in decisions to recommend to SSA which services to fund? CONCLUSIONS 1. Yes. The MDT's and Steering Committees' enabling legislation contemplates that
